title: Operators and precedence

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Operators and precedence; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: GNU C Language Introduction and Reference Manual; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Operators and precedence
sources.claim: C operators form expressions according to precedence, associativity, operand types, value computations, side effects, and sequencing rules.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C operators form expressions according to precedence, associativity, operand types, value computations, side effects, and sequencing rules.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: The expression a + b * c groups multiplication first, while parentheses communicate and enforce a different intended grouping. Your goal is to trace expressions and add grouping that makes evaluation intent unambiguous. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Translation unit and main; pf state expressions; Scalar types and declarations. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C operators form expressions according to precedence, associativity, operand types, value computations, side effects, and sequencing rules. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Precedence does not specify every evaluation order, signed overflow is undefined, and modifying and reading one scalar without required sequencing can be undefined. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Higher precedence means an operand is always evaluated earlier in time than every lower-precedence operand.

includes: Use arithmetic, relational, logical, conditional, increment and assignment operators deliberately.
Trace expressions and add grouping that makes evaluation intent unambiguous.
excludes: Precedence does not specify every evaluation order, signed overflow is undefined, and modifying and reading one scalar without required sequencing can be undefined.

outcomes: Trace expressions and add grouping that makes evaluation intent unambiguous.

examples.title: Operators and precedence: defined case
examples.situation: The expression a + b * c groups multiplication first, while parentheses communicate and enforce a different intended grouping.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: The expression a + b * c groups multiplication first, while parentheses communicate and enforce a different intended grouping.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Use precedence to determine grouping, not general evaluation order; rely only on documented sequencing and avoid multiple unsequenced accesses with side effects.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Use precedence to determine grouping, not general evaluation order; rely only on documented sequencing and avoid multiple unsequenced accesses with side effects.

examples.title: Operators and precedence: boundary case
examples.situation: A condition ready && use_value() avoids the function call when ready is zero because logical AND sequences and short-circuits its right operand.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A condition ready && use_value() avoids the function call when ready is zero because logical AND sequences and short-circuits its right operand.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Precedence does not specify every evaluation order, signed overflow is undefined, and modifying and reading one scalar without required sequencing can be undefined.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Higher precedence means an operand is always evaluated earlier in time than every lower-precedence operand.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace expressions and add grouping that makes evaluation intent unambiguous.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Higher precedence means an operand is always evaluated earlier in time than every lower-precedence operand.
misconceptions.correction: Use precedence to determine grouping, not general evaluation order; rely only on documented sequencing and avoid multiple unsequenced accesses with side effects.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: Higher precedence means an operand is always evaluated earlier in time than every lower-precedence operand.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Operators and precedence: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Precedence does not specify every evaluation order, signed overflow is undefined, and modifying and reading one scalar without required sequencing can be undefined.

recap.summary: You should now be able to trace expressions and add grouping that makes evaluation intent unambiguous. Keep this boundary in view: Precedence does not specify every evaluation order, signed overflow is undefined, and modifying and reading one scalar without required sequencing can be undefined.
